text:
eminently out of a 1976 case involving a homosexual prison inmate, convicted of an act

of terrorism. In that case, Lebach, the German Constitutional Court found that it would

be a violation of the inmate’s “personality” rights to broadcast a made-for-T.V. movie

about him. Lebach has been regarded since as the font of late twentieth-century

“personality” doctrine. 180 French law too has made strong efforts to guarantee the

privacy of accused persons as a fundamental aspect of the presumption of innnocence, 181

and more broadly of the “honorability” of the accused. 182 In line with this, both Germany

and France make considerable efforts to guarantee that prison inmates will enjoy

protections for their privacy, in ways that are unimaginable for Americans. 183 Accused

persons too enjoy, at least in principle, protections that are not available to their

American counterparts.

VI

180
    On Lebach, see Friedrich Kübler, ed., Medienwirkung and Medienverantwortung (Baden-Baden:
Nomos, 1975). The Lebach doctrine has now been altered somewhat by the so-called Lebach II decision, 1
BvR 348/98 vom 25.11.1999. These are not mechanically applied principles, of course. See e.g. Bverf G
Beschl. v. 16.5.2002—1BvR 2257/01, in NJW 2002, 3231 (dossier compiled during investigation may be
kept even after acquittal of defendant under some circumstances.) For a survey of the current state of
German law with regard to the accused at trial, see Otto Kissel, ed., Gerichtsverfassungsgesetz, 3d ed.
(Munich: Beck, 2001), § 169, para 14-20, 26 (dangers of exposure for accused); § 169, para 85-93 (press
coverage); §§171 a – 171b (closing of trials,including for protection of privacy). Germans feel the tension
between the imperative of the public openness of the courts and the need for the protection of personality
quite acutely. See Bodo Pieroth, Gerichtsöffentlichkeit und Persönlichkeitsschutz, in Hans-Uwe Erichsen,
Helmut Kollhosser and Jürgen Welp, eds., Recht der Persönlichkeit (Berlin: Duncker und Humblot, 1996),
249-277. For the law of press reporting, which certainly does leave room for much detailed publication, as
readers of German newspapers will know, see also Karl Egbert Wenzel, Das Recht der Wort- und
Bilderberichterstattung (Cologne: Schmidt, 1994), 448-449.
181
    Code Civil art. 9-1, as amended in 1992. See the fuller account in Pierre Kayser, La Protection de la Vie
Privée par le Droit, 3d ed. (Aix-Marseilles : Economica, 1995), 173-175.
182
    Jean Dematteis and Nadein Poulet-Gibot Leclerc, Peut-on Supprimer l’Article 11 du Code de Procédure
Pénale relatif au secret de l’instruction, La Semaine Juridique G 2002, I 170 para. 5. This article describes
the real tension between this ideal and the ideals of freedom of the press. For the strong contrast with U.S.
law, see esp. Paul v. Davis, 424 U.S. 693 (1976) (permitting public posting of photograph of “active
shoplifter” against whom charges had been dropped.)
183
    Whitman, Harsh Justice
